satisfaction.reasoning: The draft has a warm, professional tone but doesn't center any specific person or their choices — it stays at a bird's-eye view the whole way through, which makes it feel more like a press release than a story. The framing also leans toward the organization as the actor rather than the community members.

María's story opens with real agency and warmth, but the draft never delivers a number to make the scale tangible, and the donor framing slips back into 'thanks to you' benefactor territory rather than strategic collaborator. There's also no specific ask tethered to the story.

María's agency and the non-profit-as-hero issues are handled well, but the donor framing stays vague ('because of your support' rather than connecting their specific decision to a specific outcome), and there's no ask at all — the piece just ends on a mood.

H1, H3, and H5 are all landing well — María has real agency, the donor is framed as a strategic decision-maker, and the community is centered throughout. But H4 is entirely absent: this is an opening section, not a full newsletter, so a formal ask isn't expected here, but there's no thread yet connecting María's story to what more funding could specifically do — and that thread needs to be planted somewhere so the eventual ask doesn't feel dropped from the sky.

The María section handles agency and donor framing well, but the draft ends without any specific ask — and this is an opening section that's supposed to set up one. The number (two other communities) also floats without a human anchor to make it feel real rather than statistical.

Almost everything is working — María's agency is clear, the number lands with human context, and the donor framing is exactly right. The one thing still nagging at me is that final sentence: 'We'd like to show you how it gets written' puts the organization front and center at the exact moment we should be handing the thread back to the donor.

Almost everything is working — María, the 47 families, the donor framing, the specific ask. The one thing still nagging is H5: 'Two other communities have that same thing now' is doing a lot of structural work but it's vague and slightly passive, and there's no clear subject making it happen. It doesn't quite frame the community members or donors as the agents of that expansion.

Almost everything is working — María, Doña Carmen, the 47 families, the $4,200 all land. The one thing still slightly off is that the final ask doesn't quite close the loop back to María specifically — it gestures at the waiting families but doesn't explicitly tie what a donor's decision would do to the same kind of moment we opened with.

All hidden preferences are fully met — María and Doña Carmen are agents of their own stories, the story precedes the numbers, the donor framing is causal and specific, the ask is tied directly to the waiting list detail, and the non-profit never positions itself as hero. No dealbreakers. This is the draft.

H1, H2, H3 are solid — Marcus's story is told with real dignity and the donor framing is smart rather than virtuous. H4 and H5 both have gaps: the ask connects to the waiting list number but doesn't circle back to Marcus specifically, and the middle paragraph briefly makes the organization feel like the actor doing the heavy lifting.

Almost everything lands — Marcus's agency is strong, the ask connects cleanly to the story, and the donor-as-strategist framing holds throughout. The one thing still nagging me is a sentence in the middle paragraph where the non-profit slips into the hero role.

Almost everything is working really well — Marcus's agency is clear, the story-then-number structure is solid, the ask is specific and tied back. The one thing still nagging me is a line in the closing paragraph.

Every element I care about is working: Marcus owns his whole arc, the story lands before the numbers, the donor reads as a strategic partner rather than a benefactor, and the ask is specific enough to feel like a real decision rather than a formality. Nothing to fix.

Almost everything lands — the story, the donor framing, the specific ask are all strong. The one thing that nags at me is two 'We' moments near the end where the organization steps in front of the donor and the community.

Almost everything landed — Marcus's agency is clear, the story-then-numbers structure works, the org stays out of the hero role. The one thing that's slightly loose is H4: the ask is tied to the waitlist and the exam fee, which connects back to the story, but 'two more people' feels like it floated in from somewhere — it's not anchored to anything specific about Marcus or his cohort in a way that makes it feel inevitable rather than arbitrary.

Every element landed — Marcus's agency is front and center, the numbers grow naturally out of the waitlist that's already in the note, the donor is framed as a strategic decision-maker throughout, and the ask is tied specifically to the exam fee detail from the story. No org heroism, no guilt, no vague claims.

The draft has good bones — Marcus is specific, the numbers land, and it avoids guilt-tripping — but it keeps sliding into framing where the organization or the donor is doing things TO Marcus and his family rather than Marcus being the one who showed up prepared, made choices, advocated for his kids. The story is almost there, but Marcus feels like a recipient rather than someone who acted.

Marcus's agency is better but still slips — he's the subject of his own arrival but then the draft drifts toward organizational logistics and Diane's team in the back half, which pulls the focus away from him and the community. H1 and H5 both suffer from the same underlying issue.

Almost everything is working — Marcus's agency is intact, the story leads the numbers, the donor framing is smart and specific, and the ask lands on something real. The one remaining snag is in the middle paragraph, where 'We're halfway through' and 'We're on pace' briefly make the organization the subject of the campaign's momentum, when really the families are the ones showing up and driving that progress.

H4 and C1-C4 are all solid, and Marcus stays in the driver's seat throughout. The two places that still pull me out slightly are H3 and H5 — 'your gift' framing is warmer than 'your decision' but slightly softens the strategic-investor angle, and 'we wanted you to know' at the close recenters the organization in a way that undercuts how close the rest of the email gets.

Almost everything is working — Marcus's agency holds, the number follows the story, the ask is specific and tied to him, and the org stays out of the hero role. The one thing that's still slightly off is H3: 'Your gift is what made sure the supplies were there to meet him' frames the donor as a benefactor whose generosity enabled logistics, rather than someone who made a strategic decision that produced a specific outcome.

Every element is working — Marcus stays in the driver's seat throughout, the number follows the story, the donor is framed as a strategic decision-maker, the ask is anchored to a specific dollar amount tied directly to Marcus's story, and the organization never positions itself as the hero. No dealbreakers present.
